text: This meeting is being recorded.
Feeling pretty good.
Happy Wednesday, Tyler.
Pump day.
We're almost there and, you know, got the holidays a week away as well, so feeling pretty good.
Absolutely.
What part of the world are you calling in from?
Yeah, man.
I live in, I was going to say Houston.
I was just there this weekend, but I actually live in Florida.
Oh, nice.
What brought you to Houston?
Yeah, one of my best friends, a lot of my friends actually moved to Houston, so we had like a huge, ugly sweater Christmas party.
So it was a lot of fun.
That's awesome.
Yeah, I grew up in like the Houston area and even went to the same high school as Jimmy Butler.
So there's, there's some.
Some cool ties here in Houston, and it doesn't matter what you're doing, you can probably find a community with whatever it is here, and also Miami, I'm sure.
Are you enjoying living in Miami?
For sure.
I've spent most of my adult years here.
I grew up in Maryland.
I went to college in North Carolina.
And then, you know, my dad's side of the family is from Florida, so I kind of took it down here to get some good weather.
There you go.
Hard to beat the Florida beaches.
Definitely better than the Gulf Coast in Houston.
Well, it's great to meet you today.
You know, I'm the only AE here at GrowthX, and I think in a lot of these interviews, I'm really just trying to, like, get to know my teammate, right?

Like, get to know your sales experience, the experience that you've had, like, building processes and systems, because this is definitely more of an entrepreneurial environment than the typical software gig.
And so I'd love to just kind of, like, open it up to you to start off, to just tell me a little bit more about, you know, that transition from...
Meta and Google and some more startup sales, like what led you to want to do that?
And also just like, tell me a little bit more about, you know, why you might be interested in growthx over your current gig at WriteSonic and we'll just keep it conversational from there.
Yeah, appreciate it.
Yeah, definitely a lot to digest.
I'm to do my best to kind of just give you a quick high level overview.
If you look at my resume, look at my LinkedIn, you'll see a lot of different experiences highlighting, of course, you know, Meta and Google.
That's where I started my career, to now recently, you know, working with startups.
To be honest with you, Tyler, if you had asked me this question a few years ago, it would be hard, right?
You know, working, know, you're fouling AE, so you get it.
But being in that spot, there is a different element that's different at that corporate AE level where processes have honestly been built up for the last five to 10 years.
And so for me, actually, GrowthX is one of the companies I was looking at in my original job search in June.
I see that they found a great individual, heard a lot of great things about you.
I had a chance to connect with Tucker as well.
I may not be saying her name right, but Maria from down in Brazil.
Okay, gotcha.
Yeah, so they've kind of been filling me in.
We've had a chance to kind of, you know, stay within contact.
But yeah, I ended up at Wrightsonic, an amazing company, been there the last six months.
And typically, I wouldn't take, you know, an interview, especially if I'm already doing well or happy.
But one, this is a company that I originally was interested in, obviously, for the fact of it being AI.
AI, just this new concept of content creation, SEO, the GEO world, and being able to mass produce.
And then three, being a part of a rocket ship, know, being a part of a team that has exponential growth, working with great leaders, and the whole not.
Yeah, for sure.
It makes total sense.
What made you want to go from, you know, like great places like Meta and Google into the startup world?
Tell me more about that.
To be completely transparent, it's location-based.
You know, I was, I lived both, and I'm sure you've been to the Bay a lot, everyone in tech has.
I lived.
I in Oakland.
I was commuting into San Francisco.
Then I was spending a little bit of time in Atlanta.
I I love a lot of those places, but unfortunately, with me being in Miami, there wasn't as many, like, offices, per se.
So I needed the role to be fully remote.
Like, my managers, they tried to work with me, but this is, like, post-COVID, you know, the Googles of the world were like, hey, like, you got to come back in.
So that kind of shifted me into the startup world.

I had three years at Google, two and a half at Facebook, and every week, every month feels like years in the startup Yeah, man, it's a different animal altogether, and, like, you know, you'll be in the startup world looking for a resource, and it's, like, you got to create it, or you got to find it on your own, whereas Google, Meta, you probably have too many resources to even look through it all at places like that.
But, no, I was just kind of personally curious why you made the switch, but, you know, location-based, they're a lot less flexible than startups are, right?
Like, I could be in Houston, I could be in...
Mexico, where my fiance's parents are from, doesn't really matter.
And then at Ritesonic, what you guys do, I'm curious just your take on like, how is it different or better than like Scrunch, Profound?
Those are competitors that we hear about a lot at GrowthX.
I haven't heard Ritesonic come up too much, but I mean, like, doesn't mean that it's a bad product or anything like that.
You know, just you're in the same sort of landscape as we are.
So I'd love your take there as well.
Yeah, and appreciate the conversational feel a lot easier.
But no, Ritesonic is awesome.
For one, we've been in business for a week.
Ritesonic's been in business for the lab.
So used to pitching, man.
Been in business for the last five years.
And it's specialized both in SEO and GEO.
And so where a lot of the Profounds, the Scrunch, just kind of give you AI search tracking, Ritesonic also can help write, produce content, site audits, keyword, analyzation, the whole not.
Where Ritesonic is a bit different from some of the old...
Other competitors is that we've invested into like an action center, helping folks not only see where they rank in AI, but what to do about it.
And so it's like, as I was talking to Tucker, I'm like, wow, this is similar, but a lot different in a lot of ways.

But the reason why I took the interview, because I've gained a tremendous amount of experience over the last six to seven months, whereas I probably wasn't even ready originally for this type of role.
But now with this experience, you know, with knocking out deals and just seeing how the startup world operates, especially in this space, I feel a lot more prepared.
Yeah, man, you could never stop learning when it comes to like the SEO, the content world.
I know you've done a lot of that on your own with your social media presence.
So, you know, you learn by doing right.
But I guess with WriteSonic in particular, what are some of like the biggest objections that come up?
Because it seems like you guys do have a lot of the pieces that, you know, most teams would look for and maybe even more than like a company like Profound or...
So I'm curious, like what, what objections typically come up and like, how do you overcome those?
For sure.
And I think one of the main objections that you see a lot of times, of course, is going to be the timing or the price.
And we even talk internally.
And I think this is where Profound actually has done a good job.
And it seems like GrowthX has done a great job as well.
It's targeting and getting in front of the right people.
When you're working at that enterprise level, the conversation is going to look a lot different from that smaller agency or that medium, even small size business.
And so the pitch is good.
The product is great.
But our biggest objections is not necessarily getting in front of the right people or that correct ICP, which is always something to sort through.
And this is a conversation that me and Tucker were having where I saw, you know, the original price, $18,000 a month.
Obviously, it's going to be different, you know, at the enterprise level where you guys are kind of adding that custom package.
But getting in front of those bigger, larger companies is not always easy.
That is a skill within itself.
And so I'm curious on how you guys are doing that.
But the biggest objective is
And we see at Risonic is the price.
And of course, you work through value, but a lot of times it's getting it in front of the right stakeholders and or will go up the chain, right?
We may be talking to a smaller local company.
Hey, do we have a national or international team?
You know, is there a particular seasonality that we're working with?

And then also just an area as an AE I'm looking to improve in is just being more skeptical, turning down those original conversations, not because I don't think it could be a great fit, but it just simply won't work.
And we don't want churn.
You know, you want to make sure this will be a good fit for the customer and putting my intentions and focus on individuals and businesses where there's going to be a stronger fit.
Yeah, for sure.
can definitely relate.
And it's a weird thing to do as a salesperson, right?
Because you're so used to trying to win business.
But it's funny how like when you try to more deeply qualify something or even like sort of say out loud, hey, I'm not sure that there is the best fit for what you're looking for here.
It almost makes the prospect lean in and like makes it easier.
Makes it easier to sell.

But, you know, there's definitely, like, things that we do really well here and things that, not that we couldn't do well, but, like, we don't really want to focus on being too custom because we want to scale, right?

And so, like, when we scale to serve more clients, especially with our delivery team and not with the product that we're launching soon, that'll probably be pretty similar to what you guys have at RightSonic.
We focus a lot of our clients on the editorial work stream, right?
Like, editorial being the foundation for SEO and GEO, content that is educational and informational in nature.
A lot of times, you know, companies are asking us a million questions about, do you do backlinks?
Do you do case studies, right?
Like, we can is the answer, but, you know, where do we want to focus our team's time and what's going to actually, like, lead to the highest ROI for the client?
These are, like, conversations that we sometimes have to slow.
Slow down to speed up for, or even just, you know, stop things before we get too entrenched and go to the many other different directions that we have.
Moving into just kind of understanding tactically, like some work that you've done on different deals.
It could be at Ritesonic, it could be at a different company.
Help me kind of understand how you position the product's value to marketing or like a go-to-market leader in a recent deal that, you know, you'd closed at any of the places you've been prior.
For sure.
mean, can keep it with Ritesonic.
Obviously, a million and ten examples at every single company, but just for relevancy, especially speaking of this, you know, content creation world, it'd just be easier to focus on Ritesonic.
So even though I've only been there for a few months, I was able to close quite a bit of a significant level of deals.
I think the biggest thing for me, maybe to give you an example, working, of course, with clients and agencies, the agencies that sell is a bit different.
You know, you're positioning it in a way to help and serve the clients when you're working at the enterprise level.
You may be working across teams, multi-threading the deals, CMOs, CEOs, et cetera.
For the agency example, Paradigm Agency, it's a deal that I closed.
And so the biggest thing that helped me out there was, one, kind of understanding what they were looking for from their client stack.
And then, two, being able to identify both the situation and the pain.
I like to use the SPICE methodology when it comes to the sales.
Identifying, you know, what's the situation?
You know, what is the pain?
You know, is there any other individual contributors?
Is there any other context?
And then, of course, we want to go ahead and get those, what do we call it, champions involved.
If there's anybody in the deal that can push us ahead, especially when it comes to creating that business case in any type of internal conversations.
Now, when it comes to Paradigm Agency, again, creating that value, but then also understanding the needs of the client.

One thing that I did really well here was talking to other members of the agencies alone, talking to the main, you know, agency director, the person that has the money, but getting that build up and that feedback.
So,
What I've seen a lot of times is when you get on these group calls and it's like eight people, folks nod their head.
And then, you know, when they talk alone, they're like, nah, we're not feeling growthx.
We're not feeling right signing.
So by me having these kind of additional or individual office hours, I was able to kind of get their questions at a deeper level, solve them, you know, create the value, get that positive buy-in.
That way, when they met collectively as a team, we were able to beat out the competition, became the vendor of choice, and were quickly able to navigate into that contract process.
Yeah, it makes sense.
So what was like the value of RightSonic in that scenario?
Like how did you position what the value to the agency paradigm was?
Yeah, very easy.
You're asking the basic questions.
What is your AI search strategy and how do you provide that to your clients?
Crickets, stutters, nothing.
They don't have that, right?
So they're in the beginning process of needing a tool or product to solve it.
And then, of course, there's going to be different levels.
You have.
Folks that are still getting educated.
And then there are folks that, hey, they know we're choosing between Profound, Scrunch, and RightSonic.
So I proved out the value, the ease of use, the ability to have out both SEO and Geo.
A lot of our competitors don't have that.
And then the level of detail, being able to show every single prompt in real time.
That's one thing RightSonic did a great job with that some of the other competitors necessarily didn't have.
No APIs, no syndications in real time, exactly what each individual prompt is saying, aggregated over time.
And then the ease of use, showing them how easy it was to add a client.
I was able to create them a free trial once they got their hands on it.
And again, I had those individual conversations.
It was only a matter of time before they were ready to pull the trigger.
Nice, nice.
Yeah, I feel like there's so much power in doing a custom demo.
And we're moving towards that here.
When I got here, we would just basically demo existing workflows.
And I've got my demo workspace set up.
So a lot of collaboration needed to get to.

a place where like for every new client, they can get a glimpse of, you know, what their world would look like on growthx and how we would create artifacts for them or create, refresh a piece of content, create a net new piece of content.
It just, you know, it's hard at first, right?
But it makes your world so much easier when you can actually help, you know, that prospect like legitimately see, okay, here's what my experience would look like using Rice Sonic, right?
Using growthx.
The trick is, the trick is then just not getting two features and functionality once you're able to create that world and, you know, being able to zoom out.
It's an interesting approach you mentioned, like setting up some of those individual calls.

Could you maybe just give me like an example of one of those individual calls you set up with someone who wasn't the decision maker, but had some influence and, you know, what you got out of that conversation?
For sure.
I can give you both positive and negative, right?
The reality is we don't win every day.
The reality is that there's some deals that just go left.
And so I was working with an organization.
This is an instance where you would call it negative, where the CMO was fully built in.
But when we had those individual conversations, a lot of the individual team members, the ICs, didn't necessarily see the full value of the product.
But we discovered the reason why they didn't, because they were the marketing team, and they simply had the wrong prompts submitted into the platform.
So to your point, the risk that you run when you do these custom demos is that the information may not be relevant.
Or you may, you know, mess up their content in this specific way.
But obviously, the plus side is if you get it right, or if you're able to get that buy-in or have that conversation, we have them understand, hey, this is just a demo.
You know, we're working through this.
Let's actually take the time to set this right.
One, you're gaining their trust.
And two, you're honestly already beginning their onboarding and implementation process.
So although originally Unstoppable Domains, the CEO was full green lights, like let's do it.
But the team, there was a disconnect.
They're going to be the ones using the product.
There were some things that were off with our individual prompts.
It's also...
Through these conversations, we were able to point out, hey, these prompts are not the most relevant and accurate, which is why you don't agree with the results.
Let's take our time to reset and refresh, and let's go through this together, and this is the process how we want to utilize this tool.
We say data in equals data out.
The system is only going to produce the information that we originally tell it.

So being able to have those kind of individual conversations helped me because without it, it just would have been, hey, we like it, but no, and I never really would have truly understood why, you know?
Yeah, for sure.
And then customer success, right?
They're going to be much happier with you than someone who's just trying to push a deal through at all costs.
And, hey, I got a CEO's green light, you know, you figure out the rest.
When it came to, like, being able to get that level of product knowledge, like, walk me through how you got there, right?
Because I know BrightSonic, they don't have a ton of AEs, and was there a process that you had to, like, help create?
Was it all ready to go when you, you know, joined?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Funny enough, I even put on my email tag, found the AE.
When I started the role, there was only two other AEs, one of them based in India and one of them based in San Francisco, Austin team.
However, the AE, kind of like this, how I interviewed him, was brought in by, he went on vacation for 40 days on my second week.
So I understood.
Is this the SF guy?
Yes.
But he earned it.
He's amazing.
He's amazing.
Yeah, I don't know how he pulled it off a bit.
You know, he deserves it, right?
He worked very hard.
So maybe one day you get a three week vacation.
I was like, man, like I've never taken longer than two in my entire sales career at a given time.
40 days.
Yeah, it was like an extended family thing.
But my point being is that when I got here, course, the first week, you know, we went through a couple of calls.
But after that, he was gone.
And obviously, the CEO was very busy.
The dynamic when it's just you and the CEO is very different.
And so if you were to ask me this originally, I wouldn't necessarily know how it works.
But now I can tell you that.
Yes, building our workflows.
We didn't have HubSpot when I started.
Created an instance where we were able to set up forecasting, pipeline, et cetera.
I'm able to develop scripts, battle cards, us against profound, scrunch, AI, jumping into conversations with engineering, obviously with customer success, and just being like an always-on individual.
I do like a healthy work-life balance.
I travel a lot.
My girlfriend's Colombian.
We go to Colombia often.
Obviously, there's a million things to do in Miami.
However, sales, at the end of the day, being proactive, learning.
You ask me, how did I learn the material?
It's a ton of YouTube videos.
I listen to myself speak.
It's like sports, right?
You got to watch your highlight tapes and answer the questions, why you did this versus why you did that.
But while even my original AE was out, I would listen to his calls as well.
And that way, when he came back, it was like a brand new person.
One month went by, a month and some change, and it felt like I've been at the company for years.
So then going forward, a lot of podcasts we would listen to, and then also just sales training.
Whether it's methodologies, objection, and different YouTube, you know, things on career within tech sales.
Like, these are the types of things that I've listened to even without working, but it just even amplified being in a new industry.
Totally.
And it's a great example.
And, like, I love hearing how you were proactive, right, even though you didn't have the number one person that could have accelerated your onboarding, right?
Like, you had to basically become that person through the resources around you.
So, help me understand how you set up one of those processes, whether it was, like, the forecasting in HubSpot or the battle cards against Profound.
Like, how did you execute on one of those?
Yeah, for sure.
I mean, I've been in sales now for almost the last six years, so I'm familiar with a healthy pipeline, forecasting, et cetera.
But I've never been in a position to set it up by myself.
I'm like, okay, this is very different.
However, you know what needs to be there and simply had the CEO purchase the license, give me the login, and then I went ahead and set it up.
I was able to implement a few workflows.
I know we do workflows here, but workflows within HubSpot.
I'm able to go ahead and make sure that our...
Automation was connected.
So when someone filled out like a lead form or like a demo request, I would automatically get the request.
Hey, this client belongs to Nigel.
Here's the contact information where the profile was automatically filled out.
Meeting book.
And then we would go in stages.
We would go from discovery.
We would go from onboarding, excuse me, discovery, trial.
Then we would go to vendor of choice, negotiation.
And then, of course, close one, close loss, et cetera, et cetera.
And so really just a lot of trial and error.
Put it together.
Let's test it.
Once it's working, that's fine.
But also now when I have my conversations with the CEO, the reason why I needed it, because I hate being in a position where someone's like, hey, Tyler, hey, Nigel, you know, what are you working on?
And you have to think about it.
I'm going like hand in those guy.
have look through my notepad.
I want to go in one place where it's fully automated, has everything built out, is pretty robust.
And it's the same process in terms of even what GrowthX does today, being able to simplify and automate information.
Absolutely.
I totally agree.
We use HubSpot here as well.
So same CRM, source of truth.
So they didn't have a CRM when you joined, Sonic?
Like they didn't have stages in a CRM either?
That was like, that was something you had to help create from scratch?
Yeah, mean, everyone's so busy, right?
I mean, we have so many leads coming in.
There's no time.
The benefit of when you bring somebody in is like, obviously, I'm new.
I have a preference.
And it's easier for me to create the CRM for a few reasons.
One, don't have as many clients that I'm working with.
But two, right, don't, yeah, that's the main thing.
If you have to do this for 300 clients, it's harder to put in everyone's information.
Where I have 30 to 50 in my first month, I can go ahead and manually put in that information.
Now it's automated afterwards.
Right, right.
Makes sense.
Talk to me about like how much of the sales cycle you have to do at Right Sonic.
Do you have to do like outbound?
Is most of it inbound?
Are you involved on the post-sale side?
No worries if you're not.
This is legitimately just like me.
For sure.
I'll be fully transparent.
So Right Sonic has a really strong brand because we've been in the space for the last five years.
So when I first joined, there was a ton of in-mail.
Whether that's a direct, hey, book a demo call, or even we hired our own agency where they would send out automated messaging.
And we would have a lot of folks that requested a demo that way.
Obviously, the challenge is not everyone's at the enterprise level.
That was the biggest issue slash objection, right?
You have a lot of folks coming in that maybe can't match that enterprise price.
So, yeah, I would be in charge of looking at that list in terms of folks who reached out and immediately getting on it.
From the moment they hit that button, I like to have a response time, of course, under a day.
But if I'm eyes on a computer, under two hours.
Now, the interesting thing, Tyler, is that if they responded through the industry, excuse me, through the agency, I will go ahead and create an actual demo account for them before the call.
And that's the part that was maybe like 10 to 20 minutes where I take their website, look up a couple of their competitors and add it into our system.
So, this is...
So new that we created that I helped advise on as well.
When I first started there, we did not have a way to create like a demo account before even speaking to somebody.
So this was a part of like the outreach process.
When it came to the bigger enterprise clients, you know, obviously I'm posting on LinkedIn, I'm reaching out, I have a big LinkedIn connection and following.
So it was mainly on the inbound side.
And then you asked about the host sale.
We did have two customer success managers.
So their time would be pretty thin.
So I would one, create the contract.
I would two, send the invoice.
And I would three, set up a dedicated Slack channel for each enterprise client business.
I would add the customer success manager, but I would still very much be involved in the post-sale process in case they needed anything.
Nice.
There's honestly a lot of similarities to here just in terms of like the different things that you have to do.
It's heavy inbound at GrowthX, which is a gift and a blessing in sales, makes life a lot easier.
And then we got a pretty lean CS team.
So
We have handoff meetings for every new client that we're bringing in.
mean, when you think about it, right, the 18K per month, that's an average ACV of over 200 grand.

So you want to make sure that first impression is as strong as possible because we allow clients to start off with this eight-week strategy sprint that's like 30 to 40K, and then they can opt out by the last week.
So if you want to get paid in the sales org here, you've got to make sure that client converts from phase one to phase two.

But we've had great conversions there, and I'm confident that that'll just get easier and easier the more that we're able to collaborate CS and sales and also set up that demo environment or even give more granular insights into what their organic traffic growth could look like.
We're starting to do more of that analysis on the sales side that our delivery team usually handles the bulk of, but this can give them a head start, right, and it can set client expectations.
We're

Next thing I would love to learn about a little is, you know, maybe let's go before WriteSonic if it's not as relevant, but what's like an outreach experiment or campaign that you had run in the past that, you know, helps you consistently book meetings?
And you can even use like your resume business if that's a better example to draw from.
Yeah, I mean, when it comes to outreach, it's science and a skill.
Obviously, having a great product and a good brand makes it a lot easier.
I'm to think through so many examples.
Well, yeah, we'll just use even my resume business.
So when it comes to outreach in general, one, of course, I'm looking at any previous connections.
Let's just use LinkedIn, for example, if I'm already connected to somebody or already within someone's network, that's great.
But when it comes to that first initial outreach, usually what I do is, one, I keep it very brief.
Two, I kind of tease them a bit more.
We may know a certain thing.
Like, for example, I'll have my team and I have a couple of VAs.
I'll say, hey, this week we want to target folks that are open to work.
They have that in their bio.
So we'll
Say something specific to what they need.
Hey, I noticed that you're open to work.
You know, this is what we do.
One, two, three, four, and five.
And also we use the strategy of if it's a fit, right?
We're not into the business of begging.
I want to make sure that there's a mutual connection and fit.
So I'll say things like if it's a fit, if it makes sense, when the time is right, or I even leave things like, hey, I have one to two new ideas that could help you with X, Z.
And so for that, this particular industry, it's like, okay, once we get on the phone with them, a lot of times, you know, they hear their experience, they hear how a program works.
I don't do the resume business as much anymore, but more kind of when I did it more heavy.
Just talk to them, right?
But getting the person on the phone in sales is always the hardest thing originally because people's time is very valuable.
And they, you know, it's a lot of scams going on.
It's scams.
It's BS.
It's just BS.
So you have to, one, be reputable and credible yourself.
That's the pre-work up front and having a good product.
But I always like to keep it very conversational, not too robotic, and then leaving the actual message with an actual.
Hey, I have one idea that can help you get 10 more interviews.
Let's talk when the time is right.
Here's my link.
Nice, nice.
And what would you do to follow up from there, typically?
You know, you send that first message, but it's rare that you get the conversion right from the first message.
Although when you build the presence, it gets a little easier, right?
Of course.
And here's where it gets interesting.
This is where having a great brand kicks in.
One, you know, they say it takes six to seven times to see something, know about something, take action.
So consistently posting.
So again, if I'm at GrowthX, consistently sharing information or keeping them within the cadence.
Number two, a lot of times I may actually send them a case study.
And this is where it gets very interesting, learning about the human psychology.
FOMO is real.
Hey, Jenny, I know we were messaging back and forth a bit last week.
We just worked with Brandon and he has 30 new interviews.
Let's get on the phone and talk.
And, you know, a lot of times it depends on how your customer base is.
And at Ritesonic, we would sometimes offer a discount for cases.
And at Resume Plug, it was easier because I know these folks.
Brandon will join the call with us to help ease things as well.
And so this is something that I did at one of my previous roles, Strappy, CMS.
And I also would do this at times at Google.
